title: SNT-TC-1A: Scope, Qualification Levels, and Written Practice
narrationScript: Every certification you hold, every exam you have graded, and every audit you have survived traces back to a document that is not a law. It is a recommended practice. In this lesson we get precise about what SNT-TC-1A is, what it is not, and why the real authority in an employer-based program is the written practice your company builds from it. Start with the nature of the document. SNT-TC-1A is published by the American Society for Nondestructive Testing as a set of guidelines for employers who qualify and certify their own NDT personnel. The operative word throughout is should, not shall. On its own, the document requires nothing of anyone. It becomes enforceable in two steps. First, a contract, a purchase order, or a referencing code invokes it. Second, the employer translates the guidelines into a written practice, a controlled company document that states exactly how people are qualified and certified at that company. When an auditor arrives, they do not grade you against the book. They grade you against your own written practice. That distinction decides arguments, so hold on to it. Next comes vocabulary, because exams and audits both turn on definitions. Qualification means the demonstrated skill, knowledge, training, and experience needed to perform the work. Certification is different. It is written testimony that an individual is qualified. Qualification is a state you reach. Certification is a document your employer signs. The certifying authority in this model is the employer, which is why employer-based certification does not travel with you when you change jobs. Know the difference between a method and a technique as well. Ultrasonic testing is a method, a whole discipline built on one physical principle. Phased array is a technique inside that method. The recommended practice offers guidelines for a long list of methods, from visual, penetrant, and magnetic particle testing on the surface side, through electromagnetic testing, to radiography and ultrasonics for volumetric work, plus specialties like acoustic emission, leak testing, magnetic flux leakage, thermal infrared testing, and vibration analysis. Now the ladder of levels. A trainee is enrolled in the program but not yet certified. Trainees work under direct supervision and do not independently perform tests, evaluate results, or write reports. A Level One technician is qualified to perform specific calibrations and specific tests and to record results, all according to detailed written instructions, with supervision close at hand. A Level One does not select the technique and does not make the accept or reject call. A Level Two can set up and calibrate equipment, conduct the test, and then do the thing that defines the level, interpret indications and evaluate them against codes and acceptance criteria. Level Twos organize and report results, and they guide trainees and Level Ones on the job. A Level Three carries the program. Level Threes are capable of establishing techniques and procedures, interpreting codes, standards, and specifications, and designating the particular method, technique, and procedure to be used on a given job. They need a practical background in materials, fabrication, and product technology, general familiarity with the other NDT methods, and they are qualified to train and examine the people coming up behind them. So where do the numbers come from, the training hours and the months of experience? The recommended practice provides tables of suggested education, training, and experience for initial qualification, organized by method and by level. The figures vary widely. A simple application may call for only a few hours of formal training, while instrument-heavy methods like ultrasonics call for substantially more, and the tables offer alternate paths that recognize college study in science or engineering. Treat the tables as the engineered starting point. The employer adopts them, or modifies them with documented justification, in the written practice. Experience is typically counted in hours of actual method work, and time can sometimes count toward more than one method when the work genuinely overlaps. Candidates should satisfy the education, training, and experience recommendations before they sit for certification examinations, not after. Finally, training programs themselves. Training should be organized, not casual. That means a syllabus tied to a published topical outline, like the body of knowledge this course follows, instructors who know the material, and documentation that the training actually happened. In-house classes, outside agencies, and structured self-study can all work if the written practice says so and records prove it. By the end of this lesson you should be able to explain why the written practice, not the recommended practice, is the document your program lives and dies by, and exactly what each level on the ladder is trusted to do.
imagePrompt: A senior NDT Level III in a navy work shirt reviews a thick written practice binder with a younger technician at a conference table in a steel fabrication shop office, weld test plates and a hard hat resting beside the paperwork.

sections.heading: A Recommended Practice, Not a Law
sections.body:
SNT-TC-1A is a **recommended practice** published by ASNT: a set of guidelines an employer uses to build its own program for qualifying and certifying NDT personnel. Its scope is exactly that — guidance for **employer-based** qualification and certification — and its characteristic verb is **should**. By itself the document obligates no one. It acquires force in two steps: a contract, purchase order, or referencing code invokes it, and the employer converts its guidelines into a controlled **written practice** that states how qualification and certification actually work at that company.

The guidelines cover the personnel side of a long list of test methods. Current editions address the major industrial methods — **visual (VT), liquid penetrant (PT), magnetic particle (MT), electromagnetic/eddy current (ET), ultrasonic (UT), and radiographic (RT) testing** — along with specialties such as acoustic emission, leak testing, magnetic flux leakage, neutron radiography, thermal/infrared testing, guided wave, laser methods, and vibration analysis. Keep the vocabulary straight: a **method** is a discipline built on a physical principle (ultrasonic testing), while a **technique** is a specific way of applying it (phased array). The recommended practice organizes qualification by method; a written practice may further limit a certification to particular techniques.

For the Basic examination, the load-bearing fact is the document's status: guidance that the employer tailors, adopts, and is then audited against.

sections.heading: Definitions That Decide Arguments
sections.body:
A handful of defined terms carry the entire employer-based model, and exam questions probe whether you keep them separate.

- **Qualification** — the demonstrated combination of skill, knowledge, training, and experience required to perform NDT properly. It is a *state you reach*, supported by evidence.
- **Certification** — *written testimony* that an individual is qualified. It is a document, issued and signed by the certifying authority.
- **Certifying authority / employer** — in this model, the employer certifies. That is why an employer-based certification ends with employment and does not transfer to the next company, even though the underlying training and experience records do.
- **Written practice** — the employer's controlled procedure describing how it qualifies and certifies personnel: levels, methods, education/training/experience requirements, examinations and grading, vision requirements, and recertification rules.
- **Outside agency** — a company or individual (such as a consultant Level III or training school) that provides services like training or examinations on the employer's behalf; responsibility for certification stays with the employer.
- **Training** vs. **experience** — training is an organized program of instruction in the principles and practice of a method; experience is actual work time in the method, typically counted in hours.

When a dispute starts with 'but he's certified,' the first question a Level III asks is: *by whom, to what written practice, and with what documented qualification behind it?*

sections.caption: How a recommended practice becomes a binding program: guidelines flow into the employer's written practice, which becomes the auditable requirement once invoked by contract or code.

sections.heading: The Four Rungs: Trainee Through Level III
sections.body:
The recommended practice describes levels of qualification that divide responsibility by demonstrated capability.

- **Trainee** — enrolled in the program but not yet certified. Works only under the direct supervision of certified personnel and does not independently perform tests, interpret, evaluate, or report results.
- **Level I** — qualified to perform **specific calibrations, specific tests, and specific evaluations** according to detailed written instructions, and to record results. A Level I receives instruction and supervision from a Level II or III, does not select the technique, and does not independently judge acceptance. Some written practices add a **limited** certification (for example, a Level I limited to one automated technique on one product line) with correspondingly reduced training.
- **Level II** — qualified to **set up and calibrate equipment** and to **interpret and evaluate** results with respect to applicable codes, standards, and specifications. The Level II must be thoroughly familiar with the scope and limitations of the method, exercises on-the-job guidance over trainees and Level I personnel, and organizes and reports test results.
- **Level III** — capable of **establishing techniques and procedures; interpreting codes, standards, specifications, and procedures; and designating** the particular method, technique, and procedure for a job. The Level III needs a practical background in materials, fabrication, and product technology; general familiarity with the other methods; and is qualified to **train and examine** personnel for certification.

sections.caption: The qualification ladder: each level adds responsibility, from supervised assistance to full program authority.
Responsibility split by level under the recommended practice's model.
rows: Interpret and evaluate results to codes/criteria
Set up equipment and prepare techniques
Establish procedures; interpret codes; designate method/technique
Guides trainees and Level I on the job
Yes — qualified to train and examine

sections.heading: The Written Practice: Your Program's Constitution
sections.body:
The **written practice** is the employer's controlled document describing how it qualifies and certifies NDT personnel. At minimum a sound written practice defines: the levels and methods covered; education, training, and experience requirements for each; how examinations are administered and graded, and by whom; vision examination requirements and frequency; rules for recertification, interrupted service, termination, and reinstatement; and the records kept to prove all of it.

Two properties make this document the center of gravity. First, the recommended practice explicitly expects the employer to **review the guidelines and modify them as necessary** to meet its technical needs — but modifications belong *in the written practice*, justified and documented, not in someone's head. Second, once a purchase order or code invokes SNT-TC-1A, the written practice is the **auditable requirement**: customers and auditors verify that your program does what your own document says it does. A program that follows the book but contradicts its own written practice fails an audit; a program that departs from the book with documented justification in its written practice can pass. The Level III typically drafts and maintains this document, which is why this module sits at the front of Level III preparation.

sections.title: Audits grade you against your own document
sections.body: SNT-TC-1A supplies **guidelines**; the employer's **written practice** turns them into requirements. Once invoked by contract or code, the written practice — including any documented modifications — is the benchmark every certification in the shop is judged against.

sections.heading: Initial Qualification: Education, Training, Experience — and Real Training Programs
sections.body:
Before anyone sits for a certification examination, the candidate should satisfy the written practice's **education, training, and experience** requirements for the level and method. The recommended practice supplies tables of suggested values, organized by method and level, as the engineered starting point. The pattern matters more than memorizing numbers: **formal training hours scale with the complexity of the method** (a few hours can suffice for simple visual applications, while instrument-heavy methods such as ultrasonic testing call for substantially more at Level I and again at Level II), and the tables credit **formal education** — a candidate with engineering or science coursework may follow an alternate path with reduced hours. **Experience** is generally counted in hours of actual method work; where duties genuinely overlap, time may count toward more than one method when the written practice allows it.

**Training programs** should be organized and documented, not incidental: a syllabus keyed to a published topical outline (such as the ASNT topical outlines this course follows), instruction by people competent in the material, completion records, and examinations that verify learning. In-house courses, outside agencies, and structured self-study formats are all acceptable when the written practice recognizes them and the documentation proves attendance and content. An undocumented course, however good, contributes nothing at audit time.

checkQuestions.question: A purchase order invokes SNT-TC-1A. An auditor finds the employer's UT Level II training hours differ from the values suggested in the recommended practice. The program is acceptable if:
options: The hours exactly match the recommended practice, since the purchase order makes its tables mandatory
The employer's written practice documents and technically justifies the modified requirements
The technicians hold certifications from a previous employer with higher hours
The Level III verbally approved the reduction before training began
checkQuestions.explanation: SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice: the employer may tailor its guidelines, but the modifications must be documented and justified in the written practice, which is what the auditor verifies against.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly separates qualification from certification?
options: Qualification is the written document; certification is the demonstrated skill behind it
Qualification is demonstrated skill, knowledge, training, and experience; certification is the employer's written testimony of that qualification
They are interchangeable terms for passing the three examinations
Qualification comes from ASNT while certification comes from the employer's customer
checkQuestions.explanation: Qualification is a state you reach and can demonstrate; certification is the document — written testimony by the certifying authority (the employer in this model) — that attests to it.

checkQuestions.question: The defining capability that separates a Level II from a Level I is the authority to:
options: Record test results on a report form
Perform specific calibrations according to written instructions
Interpret indications and evaluate them against applicable codes and acceptance criteria
Designate which NDT method will be used on a new product line
checkQuestions.explanation: A Level I performs and records per written instructions but makes no acceptance call; interpretation and evaluation to codes and criteria is the Level II's defining addition. Designating methods belongs to the Level III.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of responsibilities belongs to the Level III under the recommended practice's model?
options: Performing all production testing personally and countersigning every report
Establishing techniques and procedures, interpreting codes and specifications, designating the method and technique for a job, and training and examining personnel
Maintaining the calibration laboratory and shipping records
Approving design drawings and setting the product acceptance criteria for customers
checkQuestions.explanation: The Level III is the program-level role: establishing and approving techniques and procedures, interpreting codes, designating methods and techniques, and training and examining personnel — not performing every test or setting design criteria.

checkQuestions.question: Which item would you NOT expect the employer's written practice to define?
options: The levels of qualification and NDT methods the program covers
How examinations are administered and graded, and the vision examination requirements
Education, training, and experience requirements for each level and method
The dimensional design tolerances for every product the employer manufactures
checkQuestions.explanation: The written practice governs personnel qualification and certification — levels, requirements, examinations, vision, recertification, records. Product design tolerances and acceptance criteria live in drawings, codes, and procedures, not the written practice.

references: CP-105-2024 Basic Topical Outline §1.1–1.1.7 (Recommended Practice No. SNT-TC-1A)
